result.title: Design a Flash Sale System
result.notes: Wrote complete §1-§11 HLD to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/84929a55-c466-4e23-af3a-581f842f3fdd_hld/_response.md (73,330 chars, >20K floor). Read both prompt files in full first. Wrote and ran capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python; surfaced only its real rounded numbers: 1000:1 oversubscription, ~3M buy-attempts/s vs ~100K/s single-key Redis ceiling = 30x over (the ONE decision-forcing number, flip threshold ~80K/s admitted), ~10K/s admitted (300x cut), 1000 units sell out in ~100ms, 500K reads/s -> ~500/s after 99.9% CDN hit, ~100ms p99 buy-path budget, CDN egress + burst compute as dominant cost. All 11 "## N." headings verified present. 9 mermaid flowchart TD blocks (read path, admission path, buy path, checkout path, Final union, plus 4 deep-dive mechanism diagrams for Good/Great tiers) — programmatically verified zero breaking chars (():;/"<>) in any node or edge label. Only non-mermaid fenced blocks are the 4 JSON API request/response examples in §5. No LaTeX. Deep dives center on the assigned emphasis: oversell prevention on the hot inventory counter (Bad read-modify-write -> Good atomic Lua decrement -> Great +admission+AOF+striping with failure matrix), admission control/waiting room (raw -> token bucket -> ordered virtual waiting room), reservation timeout/release with idempotent worker, plus read-your-own-writes. Includes Bad/Good/Great tiers, failure matrices, Amazon SDE-3 cost/ops lens, idempotency, the global-coordinator analysis, and 16 interviewer Q&As across all 7 domains + 60s summary. Did NOT write into data/conversations/ (orchestrator assembles JSON). capacity.py also saved in the workspace dir.

result.title: Design Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp/Tinder-style)
result.notes:
Wrote full §1–§11 HLD to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/75db5204-414d-4c67-a406-d744f3acb6d4_hld/_response.md (68,960 chars, all 11 "## N." headings present, verified by script). Capacity numbers computed by writing+running /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/75db5204-414d-4c67-a406-d744f3acb6d4_hld/capacity.py with the venv python — only rounded results surfaced in the md.

KEY COMPUTED NUMBERS (all appear in the md): peak reads ~6.9K/s; moving-entity location writes ~750K/s (3M online ÷ 4s ping); write:read ratio ~108:1 — so the WRITE firehose is the binding bottleneck, not reads (the interesting Uber/Tinder insight). THE decision-forcing number: hottest dense-city geo-cell ~30K/s combined on one shard vs a ~50K/s single-shard ceiling (0.61x), flip threshold = a cell crossing ~50K/s forces dynamic sub-celling + write-side sharding + cache. Storage is a non-issue (~0.15TB places, ~0.3GB live in RAM). Cost ~$40K/mo dominated by the always-on ingest fleet (~$30K), not the DB.

10 mermaid blocks, all flowchart TD, all labels verified free of breaking chars (: ; ( ) / < > " | etc.) and all fences balanced (script-checked). Diagrams: §6.1 read+rank, §6.2 write firehose, §6.3 place write, §6 Final union, plus Good/Great mechanism diagrams for geo-index choice, hot-cell, and moving-entity deep dives.

Structure matches system_design_prompt.md exactly: 🎬 opener + structure pitch; FRs with 🗣️/⚡ + below-the-line; NFRs with 🗣️/💥; clarifying Qs with 🗣️/↔️ fork/assumption + 🤝; §3 derivation table + flip threshold + 🎙️; §4 entities with analogy + ⚠️ probe; §5 API with JSON req/resp + security note (id from JWT); §6 per-slice decision tables + 🗣️ glosses + numbered narration + 💬 + 🎙️ + Final union + 🤝; §7 PART A/B/C storage cards; §8 Bad→Good→Great with ↩️/⚠️/🔁/🔢/✅ matrices + 🎙️ + 🧠; §9 9A-9D availability/failure/RPO-RTO/cost; §10 trade-off ledger cards; §11 all 7 domains (13 Q&A) + 60-second summary.

NOTE: per instructions I did NOT write into data/conversations/ (orchestrator assembles JSON). Minor cosmetic: capacity.py's human() helper prints "150.0BB" for the places-storage line, but the very next line states it correctly as ~0.1 TB and that number is not surfaced in the md — no impact on output.

Headline computed numbers (all from capacity.py): feed reads ~350K/s peak vs ~1K/s writes -> ~300:1 read:write ratio (the budget-shaping number); naive fan-out ~210K/s feed inserts; ONE celebrity post = ~400M feed inserts -> ~6.7M inserts/s to hit a 60s SLO (the DECISION-FORCING number that forces the push/pull hybrid, flip threshold ~hundreds of thousands of followers); likes ~175K/s peak; media ~64 PB/yr; CDN egress ~900 PB/mo ~ $18M/mo (dominant cost); feed-store miss QPS ~17K/s at 95% hit.

Structure: all 11 "## N." headings present and fully expanded. Required emoji markers used throughout (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ 🔀 ❓ ⚡ 💥 ↔️ ↩️ 🔁 🔢 ✅). §6 has 3 per-requirement slices (upload, follow, hybrid feed) each with a 5-col architecture decisions table + key-terms gloss + flowchart TD + numbered narration, plus a reconciled "Final (high-level)" union diagram. §8 has 4 deep dives (feed generation, celebrity hot-key, like counts, media/CDN) each escalating Bad->Good->Great with decision-forcing math + failure matrix on Great tiers. §7 has entity table + 4 storage decision cards + per-operation consistency table.

Mermaid: 8 fenced blocks, all flowchart TD. Static-validated — every edge label is quoted (|"..."|) and free of breaking chars ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; { }; node labels clean; cylinder [(...)] and queue [[...]] shapes used correctly; 4 subgraph titles properly quoted. No mermaid CLI in sandbox (no network) so validation was static (regex scan over all node/edge/subgraph labels) — 0 real issues.

Did NOT write into data/conversations/ (per instructions — orchestrator assembles JSON). Scratch dir contains capacity.py + _response.md only. The hld_session_builder.md's /Users/anshullkgarg paths were ignored as instructed; used /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora as root.

result.title: Design a Distributed Cache (Redis-style)
result.notes:
Wrote /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/084c563c-9047-4d34-a2b8-18502316af3c_hld/_response.md (80,908 chars, well over the 20K bar). All 11 "## N." headings present and fully expanded per the system_design_prompt.md structure (emoji markers, Bad/Good/Great deep dives, decision tables, glosses, scripts, checkpoints).

Capacity: wrote and RAN /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/084c563c-9047-4d34-a2b8-18502316af3c_hld/capacity.py with the project venv. Headline computed numbers used in §3 and deep dives: ~10M ops/s aggregate (9M reads / 1M writes), ~100K ops/s per-node ceiling -> 128 primary shards / 384 total nodes, ~12.4 TB RAM (3 copies, ~$75K/mo dominant cost), hot key ~450K QPS vs 100K ceiling = ~4-5x overload (the ONE binding bottleneck, flip threshold ~80K QPS), consistent hashing +1 node moves ~0.8% of keys vs ~99% for mod-N, stampede ~90x DB overload, failover RTO ~6s/shard, replica lag ~5ms. Cost script print showed $0.07M which equals $74,250 = ~$75K/mo (verified, consistent).

Mermaid: 12 ```mermaid blocks, all flowchart TD, all node/edge labels quoted and verified free of breaking chars ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; { }. Validated programmatically — zero label issues, all headers valid.

Deep dives center on the assigned emphasis: consistent hashing + rebalancing (mod-N -> ring -> virtual nodes/16384 slots), hot key (shard harder -> replicate hot shard -> client-side cache + invalidation), replication & failover (async manual -> Raft quorum + epoch fencing), cache stampede (plain TTL -> single-flight -> probabilistic early recompute + stale-while-revalidate). Amazon/Uber SDE-3 bar reflected: cost & ops first-class (§9D, §10 RF3-vs-RF2 card, ops-cost Q in §11), customer-degradation framing, decisive trade-off ledger.

Did NOT write into data/conversations/ (per instructions — orchestrator assembles JSON). Scratch files live in the workspace dir only.

result.title: Design a Distributed Job Scheduler
result.notes:
Complete §1-§11 HLD written to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/692baf11-4116-4073-b333-d8a8411e97b8_hld/_response.md (88,302 chars, well over the 20K floor). All 11 "## N." headings verified present. 11 mermaid blocks, all flowchart TD, all node/edge labels verified free of breaking chars ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; { } via a parser script; fences balanced.

Capacity computed by /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/692baf11-4116-4073-b333-d8a8411e97b8_hld/capacity.py (ran with the project .venv python; only rounded results surfaced in md). Key numbers used everywhere: ~250M live jobs; ~2.6K avg dispatch QPS; ~40K peak QPS in a ~2s top-of-minute herd (15x burst) — THE decision-forcing number vs ~5K/s single-partition ceiling, forcing an ~8-shard time-bucketed due-index; reading a bucket is ~1,575x cheaper than a full scan; ~1.6K workers at peak (~556 avg via autoscale); ~23 GB dedup store; ~0.6 TB durable store; ~$120K/mo dominated by worker compute. Flip threshold stated: under ~5K/s peak, drop sharding+coordinator.

Structure matches the prompt: §1 has 🎬 pitch, 5 FRs each with feature + 🗣️ + ⚡ Why-it's-hard, below-the-line list, 5 NFRs with talkable targets + 🗣️ + 💥, closing 🎙️ Script. §2 has 5 clarifying Qs (question + 🗣️ why + ↔️ If YES/NO fork + assumption) + 🤝 Checkpoint. §3 derivation table + decision number + flip threshold + 🎙️ Script. §4 five entities w/ analogy + ⚠️ probe. §5 endpoints w/ JSON req/resp, idempotency, JWT/lease-token security. §6 has 6.1-6.5 each with 5-col decision table, 🗣️ key-terms gloss, focused mermaid, numbered narration, 💬 + 🎙️; ends with reconciled Final union diagram + 🤝 Checkpoint. §7 Part A entity table, Part B four storage decision cards, Part C per-op consistency. §8 four deep dives (find-due-jobs, avoid-double-run/coordinator, worker-failure/lease, thundering-herd) escalating Bad/Good/Great with mechanism diagrams, 🔢 math, ✅ failure matrices, 🎙️ + 🧠. §9 9A-9D availability/failure-table/RPO-RTO/cost. §10 four 🔀 trade-off cards. §11 all 7 domains, 16 Q&A, + 60-second summary. No app/algo code; only JSON API examples + the run capacity.py.

findings.where: §3 Scale & Capacity, line 109 (also Trade-off Ledger lines 671-672)
findings.problem: The flip threshold for skipping the waiting room is stated circularly: 'if peak ADMITTED demand stays under ~80K/s, one Redis key is enough and I'd skip the waiting room entirely.' But 'admitted demand' only exists if you already have a waiting room/admission controller doing the admitting. If you skip the waiting room there is no throttle, so the load reaching the counter is the raw ~3M/s spike, not an 'admitted' rate. The condition for skipping admission control should be framed on RAW incoming buy QPS, not admitted QPS.
findings.fix: Reword to: 'if the RAW peak buy QPS stays under ~80K/s (i.e. the spike itself is small), one Redis key absorbs it directly and I'd skip the waiting room.' Reserve the term 'admitted demand' for the separate question of how hard to throttle once a waiting room exists.

findings.problem: Bad/Good/Great tier titles are written as '**#### Bad: ...**' — a '####' heading wrapped in bold markers. Because of the leading '**', most markdown renderers will NOT parse this as an H4 heading; it renders as literal bold text with the '####' characters visibly shown (e.g. '#### Bad: Read-modify-write in the app'). Cosmetic, but the '####' leaks into the rendered output.
findings.fix: Use either a clean heading ('#### Bad: ...') or plain bold without the hashes ('**Bad: ...**'). Don't combine '####' inside '**...**'.

findings.problem: The striping-vs-K reasoning is slightly muddled. Line 498 ties striping's necessity to 'very large K with very high admitted throughput', and line 109 says 'sharding only helps when K itself is large'. Striping the counter relieves WRITE RATE (distributing writes across keys/nodes); that benefit is a function of admitted write QPS exceeding one key's ceiling, not of K per se. Large K is merely the usual reason admitted throughput would be allowed high enough to need it. As written it conflates the count (K) with the write-rate ceiling, which is the very distinction the doc elsewhere (correctly) draws.
findings.fix: State plainly: 'Striping relieves the single-key WRITE-RATE ceiling; it becomes necessary only when admitted write QPS would exceed ~80-100K/s — which in practice only happens when K is large enough to justify admitting that many writers. For K=1,000, write rate is the constraint and admission control is the right lever, not striping.'

findings.where: §6.1 read-path diagram (line 232, node KV 'Status cache Redis') vs §9C line 645 and §6.1 narration line 243
findings.problem: Minor reconciliation gap: the read path reads coarse status from a 'Status cache Redis' object, while §9C lists the coarse status cache as 'rebuildable / recompute from counter' and step-3 narration says on a Redis blip it serves LIVE optimistically. The relationship between the status-cache Redis and the inventory-counter Redis (is the status derived from the counter? on what cadence?) is never spelled out, so how the coarse 'available/sold_out' flips to sold_out at the right moment is left implicit.
findings.fix: Add one sentence: the read service derives coarse status from the inventory counter periodically (e.g. flips to 'sold_out' when remaining hits 0, with ~1s lag) and caches it; this keeps the hot read path off the contended counter while staying eventually correct.

findings.where: §8 deep dive 3 'Great', line 611; §7 Part C, line 458; §11 Q&A line 750-753
findings.problem: Read-your-own-writes is hand-waved. The write path is async: ack 202, then the position is applied to the live in-memory index via Kafka with the doc's own stated 1-2s pipeline latency (lines 276, 442). The doc claims a returned version/ts token means 'the index serves AT LEAST that version' — but a read arriving ~100ms after the ack will hit a live index that has NOT yet applied that version, and the token alone cannot make a lagging index produce a value it doesn't hold. No mechanism (client-side local render of own position, sticky read-through to the ingest tier, or read-wait-for-version) is given to bridge the apply lag, so the RYOW guarantee is asserted, not designed. This is exactly the consistency claim the review flags as commonly hand-waved.
findings.fix: Specify the actual mechanism: e.g. the driver app renders its own position client-side (it just sent it), so server RYOW isn't needed for the self case; OR the ingest tier keeps a small per-entity 'last-acked position' cache that a self-read reads through until the async apply lands; OR a self-read carries the version and the read path blocks/long-polls until the live index reaches it. State which, and that 'serve at least version V' is only achievable via one of these, not via the token alone.

findings.problem: The structure pitch explicitly promises four deep dives — 'the geo-index choice, the hot dense-city cell, moving-entity updates, and read scaling' — and the review's stated emphasis lists 'ranking + read scaling' as a deep-dive target. Only three Bad->Good->Great deep dives are delivered (geo-index, hot cell, moving-entity). Read scaling / ranking has no dedicated deep dive; it is only touched in §6.1 and a couple of Q&A bullets. The doc sets the expectation and does not meet it.
findings.fix: Either add a fourth Bad->Good->Great deep dive on read scaling + ranking (Bad: hydrate-everything per query / no cache; Good: query cache + read replicas with the miss-QPS reasoning; Great: cell-level cache keying + tiered ranking / candidate-gen-then-pluggable-scorer with the cache-miss QPS that sizes replicas), OR fix the structure pitch to promise three deep dives and fold read-scaling explicitly under the hot-cell dive. Given reads are genuinely the small side (108:1), trimming the promise is acceptable, but the current mismatch reads as an undelivered commitment.

findings.where: §6.1 search diagram (lines 238-253) vs API §5 line 153 and final diagram line 380
findings.problem: The §6.1 per-slice search+rank diagram shows only the static Geo-Index, but the API supports type=person which 'routes to the live index' (line 153), and the final reconciled diagram adds a 'R4 live dots' edge to the Live Geo-Index (line 380). The moving-entity read is acknowledged in §6.1 prose ('moving dots always read live') but missing from the slice's own diagram, so the slice diagram is incomplete relative to its own API.
findings.fix: Add a 'scan live dots' edge from Search to the Live Geo-Index in the §6.1 diagram (or add a one-line note that the live-index read is drawn in the reconciled diagram), so the per-slice picture matches the stated dual-index read path.

findings.problem: Deep-dive subheaders are written as '**#### Bad: ...**' — the '#### ' is inside bold markers, so markdown renders the literal text '#### Bad:' in bold rather than producing an H4 heading. Cosmetic but it means the Bad/Good/Great steps are not real headings and won't appear in any TOC/outline.
findings.fix: Use a real heading without bold wrapping (e.g. '#### Bad: lat/lng columns + bounding-box SQL') or drop the '#### ' and keep just the bold label. Pick one; don't combine '####' with '**'.

findings.problem: Headline numbers all match the doc exactly (DAU 30M, peak read 6.9K/s, miss 1.4K/s, writes 750K/s, ratio 108:1, hot-cell 30K/s, flip 50K/s, cost ~$38-40K). Two cosmetic script-output quirks only: (a) the script prints static-places storage as '~0.1 TB' due to f'{...:.1f}' rounding of the true 0.15 TB, while the doc correctly says ~0.15TB — so the doc is right and the script display is the off-by-rounding one; (b) the human() helper prints '150.0BB' for 1.5e11 bytes (it appends the 'B' magnitude suffix then the literal byte 'B'). Neither affects any doc value or decision.
findings.fix: In capacity.py, print the storage TB with one extra decimal (':.2f') or compute 0.15 directly, and fix human() so byte counts don't collide with the 'B'=billion suffix (e.g. pass a unit-aware flag or format bytes separately). Doc text needs no change.

findings.problem: The variable cdn_offload is defined but never used in the cost computation. A skeptic reading the code might think the 95% edge-offload should reduce the $18M egress figure. (It correctly should NOT — CDN bills total client-facing egress regardless of edge vs origin hit; offload only reduces origin pulls.) The dangling unused variable invites that exact misreading.
findings.fix: Either delete the unused cdn_offload variable, or add a one-line comment that it models origin-pull reduction (not the billed client egress) so a reviewer doesn't think the $18M is double-counting. The $18M/900PB number itself is correct as-is.

findings.problem: The POST /v1/posts response is 202 PROCESSING but provides no way for the client to learn when the post becomes READY (no Location/status-poll URL, no webhook/push mention). The doc says transcode can mark a post FAILED (line 277) but the API gives the client no handle to observe that transition.
findings.fix: Add a status/poll affordance to the 202 response (e.g. a statusUrl or note that READY/FAILED is delivered via the realtime/notification channel). One sentence in the 'Key design decision' bullet would close it.

findings.where: §3 capacity table, 'Media ingested/year' row (line 106) vs capacity.py
findings.problem: The table derivation says 'x1.4 durability' giving ~64 PB/yr. capacity.py models this 1.4x as a generic 'replication factor for talkability' (line 92-94) while the comment notes real object-store erasure coding is ~1.4x effective overhead — so the label '1.4 durability' is slightly hand-wavy (it conflates erasure-coding overhead with replication). Numbers are internally consistent (46 PB raw -> 64.4 PB), just the one-word justification is loose.
findings.fix: Relabel as '×1.4 erasure-coding/storage overhead' rather than 'durability' so the multiplier's meaning is precise; no number change needed.

result.title: Design a Web Crawler
result.notes:
Complete HLD written to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/94edbaa4-9115-492e-bd64-45c7dc88343d_hld/_response.md (93,042 chars). All 11 "## N." headings present and fully expanded per the system_design_prompt.md structure (structure pitch, functional flows with plain-words + why-hard, NFRs with what-breaks, clarifying Qs with design forks, capacity table, core entities with analogy+probe, API with JSON examples + idempotency/security, 5 §6.x slices each with decisions table + glosses + numbered narration + script, Final union diagram, data model PARTS A/B/C, Bad/Good/Great deep dives, reliability 9A-9D, trade-off ledger, 12+ interviewer Qs across 7 domains, 60s summary).

Capacity computed by running capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python — all surfaced numbers are the rounded real outputs: ~12K fetch/s, ~700K links/s discovered, ~14K new-URL enqueues/s, Bloom 120GB (13x leaner than 1.6TB exact set), 2.3PB content storage, ~300TB metadata, ~8Gbps ingress, ~100 fetcher workers, and the decision-forcing number ~12K active domains in parallel (forces per-domain queues, flip threshold 115K at 1-per-10s politeness). Costs: $1.2M/mo proxy/IP pool (dominant), $46K/mo storage, $5K/mo fetchers.

14 mermaid blocks, all flowchart TD, fences balanced (36 total), all node/edge labels scrubbed of breaking chars — fixed two: "Quality / trap score" (slash removed) and "Hamming dist <= 3" (< removed). Subgraph titles use valid quoted form. Deep dives center on the four emphasized areas: frontier+politeness (per-domain queues, Mercator-style two-stage, min-heap selector), dedup at billions scale (sharded Bloom + simhash/LSH), distributed coordination (consistent hashing + consensus + idempotent leases), freshness/recrawl + trap avoidance. Scratch workspace (capacity.py, _response.md) created under workspace/; did not touch data/conversations/.

findings.problem: Invalid ISO-8601 timestamp: "expiresAt": "2026-06-21T10:00:60Z". The seconds field is 60, which is illegal (valid range 00–59). It looks like now (10:00:00) + 60s TTL was computed by appending 60 to the seconds field instead of rolling to 10:01:00. This is doubly awkward because the very next sentence (line 201) boasts that the server authoritatively computes the absolute expiry — an interviewer would catch this false-precision slip immediately.
findings.fix: Change the timestamp to "2026-06-21T10:01:00Z" (10:00:00 + 60s).

findings.problem: Internal inconsistency in deep-dive ordering. §3 explicitly claims the hot key 'leads my deep dives' (L137) and 'it's why hot-key mitigation is my first deep dive' (L139), and the §6.3 checkpoint (L411) offers to 'lead with the hot key.' But §8 actually orders the deep dives as: (1) consistent hashing/partitioning, (2) hot key, (3) replication/failover, (4) stampede. Hot key is the SECOND deep dive, not the first. If an interviewer says 'you told me you'd start with the hot key,' the candidate is caught contradicting their own setup.
findings.fix: Either reorder §8 so the hot-key dive comes first (consistent with the binding-bottleneck framing), or soften §3/§6.3 wording to 'it's the headline deep dive / I'll go deep on it' rather than 'my first deep dive' / 'leads my deep dives.'

findings.where: §3 capacity table, lines 124–125 + capacity.py shard count
findings.problem: Slight headroom slippage that is reconciled but worth noting: the derivation targets '+30% headroom ≈ 130' then rounds DOWN to 128 shards. 128 delivers only ~22% headroom (10M/128 = 78,125 ops/s vs the 100K ceiling), not the stated 30%. The choice is defensible (128 = power of 2, gives clean 16384/128 = 128 slots/node, and 78K is still safely under ceiling and is explicitly reconciled on L125/L502), so this is not wrong — just a small gap between the '30% headroom' phrasing and the delivered ~22%.
findings.fix: Optional: note that rounding to the power-of-two 128 trades the target 30% headroom down to ~22% (still comfortably under the 100K ceiling) for clean slot division. No numeric change required.

findings.where: §8 Deep Dive 'avoid double-running', line 600 (and the contradicting predicate at lines 365/427)
findings.problem: The 'Good' tier presents the claim SQL as: `UPDATE jobs SET lease_owner=me, lease_expiry=now+30s WHERE job_id=X AND status='SCHEDULED' AND lease_expiry < now()`. This is a genuine SQL correctness bug: a never-leased SCHEDULED job has lease_expiry = NULL, and in SQL `NULL < now()` evaluates to NULL (not TRUE), so the row is excluded. As written, this predicate would NEVER claim a fresh job — the common case. It also contradicts the other two places (lines 365 and 427) which use `status=SCHEDULED AND lease IS NULL`. The two predicates are presented as the same anti-double-run primitive but are not equivalent, and one is wrong. A senior interviewer would catch this immediately in the core correctness mechanism.
findings.fix: Use a predicate that handles both fresh and expired leases: `WHERE status='SCHEDULED' AND (lease_expiry IS NULL OR lease_expiry < now())`. Make the predicate identical everywhere it appears (lines 365, 427, 600) so the claim/cancel-race reasoning stays consistent.

findings.problem: Only the first deep dive (find-due-jobs) has all three tiers (Bad/Good/Great, lines 547/554/569). The other three deep dives — double-run, worker-death, thundering-herd — have only Good→Great; the explicit 'Bad' tier is omitted. The prescribed §8 structure is 'Bad→Good→Great'. The progression is still readable (each 'Good' tier states 'What breaks' / 'What forces the upgrade', so it functions as the flawed baseline), but the prescribed three-tier label is not fully met for 3 of 4 dives.
findings.fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Bad' tier to the double-run, worker-death, and thundering-herd dives (e.g. for double-run: 'Bad: just dequeue and run — two pollers read the same due row and both run it, classic double-run'), or relabel the existing 'Good' as the baseline if a two-tier shape is intended.

findings.problem: The reduction-factor row shows the rounded value '~1,575x fewer' but the talkable phrase says 'a thousand-x cheaper' while §6.3/§8/§11 say '~1,500x'. The spoken phrase ('a thousand-x') understates the computed/cited figure and is slightly inconsistent with the '~1,500x' used elsewhere. Cosmetic only — arithmetic (1575x, confirmed by capacity.py) is correct.
findings.fix: Align the talkable phrase to 'about fifteen-hundred-x cheaper' to match the rounded value and the rest of the document.

findings.problem: The doc emphasizes idempotency on mutating endpoints, but only POST /v1/files/commit carries an idempotencyKey. POST /shares and POST /restore are mutating but have no idempotency key and no retry-safety note. A retried /shares could create a duplicate share row; a retried restore could create an extra version. The §11 idempotency answer (line 913) only covers commit.
findings.fix: Add an idempotencyKey (or natural-key dedup, e.g. unique (resource_id, grantee_id) for shares) to /shares and /restore, or add one line stating shares are upserted by (resource,grantee) and restore is naturally idempotent because the resulting version is deterministic.

findings.problem: chunks_per_change = round(2MB / 4MB) = round(0.5) = 0, then max(1,0) = 1. So the '2 MB net new bytes per change' assumption (line 44) is inert — it never actually affects chunk count; every change is modeled as exactly 1 chunk regardless of the 4 MB chunk size. The two assumptions don't interact, which is a slightly misleading derivation chain even though the headline 290K→174K numbers are unaffected.
findings.fix: Either drop the unused avg_changed_bytes assumption and state 'model ~1 changed chunk per change event,' or pick a changed-bytes figure (e.g. 6 MB) that genuinely yields >1 chunk so the chunk-size assumption does work.

findings.problem: read:write computed as 20/50 = 0.4 and labeled '0.4:1 (close to balanced — sync is write-heavy)'. 0.4:1 means writes outnumber reads 2.5:1 — it is markedly write-heavy, not 'close to balanced.' The parenthetical is self-contradictory. Low blast radius: this label lives only in capacity.py and is not surfaced in §3 of the doc.
findings.fix: Relabel as '0.4:1 — distinctly write-heavy (more change events than read pulls), unusual for storage and worth calling out.'

findings.problem: The §8 header promises Bad → Good → Great, and the first three deep dives follow it, but the fourth deep dive jumps straight to 'Good: shard by user_id' with no 'Bad' tier (e.g. a single unsharded primary). Minor structural inconsistency with the prescribed pedagogy.
findings.fix: Add a short 'Bad: one unsharded metadata DB' tier (caps ~15K writes/s, dies at 580K/s) to match the Bad→Good→Great pattern of the other three.

findings.problem: Chunk bytes are served via GET /v1/chunks/{hash} → 302 to a CDN/presigned URL. The doc claims authz on 'every read' (line 399) and the §11 security answer only defends against guessing file_id at the metadata layer. It never explicitly closes the content-addressed-store gap: whether knowing/guessing a raw chunk hash lets a user fetch bytes they have no permission for. It is largely mitigated (hashes come only from authz-gated manifests; presigned URLs are short-lived), but the loop isn't stated.
findings.fix: Add one sentence: chunk hashes are only obtainable via an authz-checked manifest read, and presigned/CDN URLs are short-lived and per-request, so chunk-hash knowledge alone does not grant access.

findings.problem: The 12 Bad/Good/Great tier headers are written as `**#### Bad: ...**` — an ATX heading marker (`####`) embedded INSIDE bold markers on the same line. In CommonMark a `#` only starts a heading when it is the first non-space character of the line; here it is preceded by `**`, so it does NOT render as a heading. It renders as literal bold text with visible stray `#### ` hashes in front of each tier name (e.g. reader sees '#### Bad: One global priority queue'). Cosmetic but visible on every deep-dive subheading.
findings.fix: Drop the `####` from inside the bold, or move it to line-start. Either `**Bad: One global priority queue**` (bold only) or `#### Bad: One global priority queue` (real heading, no surrounding `**`). Apply to all 12 tier headers.

findings.problem: Minor rounding optimism in the headline framing: script computes the steady fetch rate as 11,574/s and active-domains-needed as 11,574, both consistently rounded UP to '~12K' throughout. Arithmetic is correct and self-consistent (verified against capacity.py), but the decision-forcing number is repeatedly stated as '12K' when the derived value is 11.6K. Not wrong, just a slight upward round presented as the exact driver. No flip-direction error and the flip threshold (115K at 1-per-10s) is correct.
findings.fix: Optional: state it as '~11.6K, call it 12K' once so the round is explicit, or leave as-is — the rounding is within normal talkable tolerance and does not affect any decision.

result.summary:
Fixed the MAJOR SQL correctness bug in the lease-claim predicate and made it identical in all three places it appears.

Issue 1 [MAJOR] — claim predicate was wrong and inconsistent:
- §8 Deep Dive "Good" tier (line 600): the claim SQL used `WHERE ... AND status='SCHEDULED' AND lease_expiry < now()`. A never-leased SCHEDULED job has lease_expiry = NULL, and in SQL `NULL < now()` is NULL (not TRUE), so a fresh job — the common case — would NEVER be claimed. Replaced with `status='SCHEDULED' AND (lease_expiry IS NULL OR lease_expiry < now())` and added an inline note explaining the NULL arm claims fresh jobs and the `< now()` arm reclaims expired leases.
- §6.3 dispatch narration (line 365): previously `status=SCHEDULED AND lease IS NULL` — only handled fresh jobs, not expired-lease reclaim, and was textually different from line 600. Unified to the same `status='SCHEDULED' AND (lease_expiry IS NULL OR lease_expiry < now())`.
- §6.5 track/cancel race reasoning (line 427): previously `status=SCHEDULED AND lease IS NULL`. Unified to the same predicate; clarified the cancel wins because status is no longer SCHEDULED.

All three now use the byte-identical predicate so the claim / cancel-race reasoning is consistent. Line 549 (the "Bad" tier full-table scan, `next_run <= now() AND status='SCHEDULED'`) is intentionally not a lease claim and correctly left alone.

capacity.py: NOT changed — this was a SQL correctness/consistency bug, no arithmetic was wrong, so no re-run was needed.

Mermaid: re-scanned all 11 blocks. No breaking characters (parens, colons, semicolons, slashes, quotes, angle/curly/square brackets) inside any node label or edge text. The only double-quotes present are the standard, renderer-safe subgraph-title quoting (`subgraph X["Plain words"]`) with plain-word titles. No mermaid edits were required by this finding; count unchanged.

Invariants preserved: all 11 sections intact and fully expanded; md = 88,767 chars (> 20,000); no LaTeX. File: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/692baf11-4116-4073-b333-d8a8411e97b8_hld/_response.md

Fixed both actionable defects in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/75db5204-414d-4c67-a406-d744f3acb6d4_hld/_response.md. capacity.py needed no change — its math was already correct and matches every documented number (verified by re-running with the venv python: 1.4K/s miss QPS, 8K/replica, 108:1, 30.3K/s hot cell).

ISSUE 1 (RYOW hand-wave) — replaced the asserted "version token guarantees self-consistency" claim everywhere with a real, designed mechanism, explicitly acknowledging the async apply path lags the ack by the doc's own 1-2s pipeline latency so a token alone cannot make a lagging index serve a value it doesn't hold. Specified three concrete mechanisms in priority order: (1) client-side self-render of the just-sent position (primary — server RYOW not needed for self), (2) an ingest-tier per-entity last-acked-position cache written synchronously on the 202 that self-reads read through until the async apply lands (this is what actually makes "serve at least version V" true), (3) read-wait/long-poll for version as a last resort. Updated all four touchpoints: §8 deep-dive-3 "Great" approach (line ~608), its failure-matrix row, its mermaid diagram (now shows Ingest tier + synchronous Last-acked cache + client self-render + read-through), its Script; §7 Part C consistency table (line ~458); §11 Q&A "Can a driver see their own pin lag behind?" (lines ~750-753).

ISSUE 2 (undelivered 4th deep dive) — added a full Bad->Good->Great deep dive on read scaling + ranking in §8: Bad = hydrate-everything/no-cache; Good = query cache + read replicas sized from the ~1.4K/s miss QPS (1 warm + 1 cold replica, matching capacity.py); Great = cell-resolution cache keying + tiered candidate-gen-then-pluggable-scorer (distance/rating/popularity/open-now blend), with decision-forcing math, a failure matrix, two new mermaid diagrams, a Script, and a follow-up. Tightened the structure pitch (line 5) and the §6 checkpoint to firmly promise the four deep dives, removing the "three or four"/optional framing — the commitment is now met.

All 11 sections intact and expanded; no LaTeX (the only "$" are currency cost figures); all mermaid labels renderer-safe (no parens/colons/semicolons/slashes/angle/curly brackets in label or edge text — the only quotes are standard subgraph-title delimiters, pre-existing).
